title: Functions
overview: Functions are the building blocks of organized code. They let you package logic into reusable units — write once, use anywhere.

blocks.title: Why Do We Need Functions?
blocks.body: Without functions, you copy-paste the same code everywhere. Functions let you **define once, use many times**.
bullets: Make programs easier to read and understand
Fix bugs in one place instead of everywhere
Break complex problems into smaller pieces

blocks.title: A Function is Like a Vending Machine
blocks.text: You press A3 (parameters), the machine runs its code (function body), and out comes a snack (return value). You don't care HOW it works inside — just press the button and get the result.

blocks.title: Without Functions vs With Functions
blocks.code:
# WITHOUT functions:
print("Hello, Alice!")
print("Hello, Bob!")
print("Hello, Charlie!")

# WITH functions:
def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Alice")
greet("Bob")
greet("Charlie")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Hello, Alice!
Hello, Bob!
Hello, Charlie!
Hello, Alice!
Hello, Bob!
Hello, Charlie!

blocks.title: How to Define a Function
blocks.body: Use `def` followed by the function name, parentheses for parameters, a colon, and an indented body.
bullets: Parameters go inside parentheses: def add(a, b):
Use return to send a value back
If no return, the function returns None

blocks.title: Functions with and without Return
blocks.code:
def say_hello():
    print("Hello!")

result = say_hello()
print(f"Return value: {result}")

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)
print(f"3 + 4 = {result}")

blocks.title: Multiple Parameters
blocks.code:
def describe_person(name, age, city):
    return f"{name} is {age} years old from {city}"

print(describe_person("Alice", 25, "New York"))
print(describe_person("Bob", 30, "London"))
blocks.expectedOutput:
Alice is 25 years old from New York
Bob is 30 years old from London

blocks.title: Default Parameters
blocks.body: You can give parameters **default values**. If the caller doesn't provide that argument, the default is used.
bullets: Parameters with defaults must come LAST

blocks.title: Default Parameters
blocks.code:
def greet(name="Guest", greeting="Hello"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}!"

print(greet())
print(greet("Alice"))
print(greet("Bob", "Hi"))
blocks.expectedOutput:
Hello, Guest!
Hello, Alice!
Hi, Bob!

blocks.question: What happens if you put a parameter with a default BEFORE one without? Like def add(a=5, b):
blocks.answer: Python gives a SyntaxError! Parameters with defaults must come AFTER those without.

blocks.title: Area Calculator
blocks.instructions: Define rectangle_area(length, width) and test with two different pairs of values.
blocks.solution:
def rectangle_area(length, width):
    return length * width

print(rectangle_area(5, 3))
print(rectangle_area(7, 4))
blocks.hint: Use return length * width. Indent the function body with 4 spaces.

keyTakeaways.text: Functions let you write code once and reuse it
Use def to define, return to send back a value
Parameters are inputs; return values are outputs
Break complex programs into small, reusable functions

whatYouLearned: Why functions are essential for organized code
How to define functions with def
How to use parameters and return values
How to create functions with default parameters
How to build reusable calculation functions
